subject: ap-chemistry
title: NH₂Cl vs NCl₃: Solubility, IMFs & Vaporization
stimulus: Answer the following questions about the compounds $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ and $\text{NCl}_3$. The Lewis electron-dot diagrams of the two compounds are shown.

parts.prompt: Calculate the number of moles of $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ (molar mass 51.48 g/mol) present in 1.0 L of a solution in which the concentration of $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ is 0.0016 g/L.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated number of moles: 1 point
wrong_examples: Dividing molar mass by mass (inverted): $51.48 / 0.0016$ — must divide mass by molar mass, not the reverse.
Not multiplying g/L by L before dividing — must find total grams in the 1.0 L volume first.
common_traps: $n = m/M$. Total mass = concentration (g/L) × volume (L) = 0.0016 g. Then divide by molar mass to get moles.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For a correct calculated value: 1 point. $1\text{ L} \times (0.0016\text{ g/L}) / 51.48\text{ g/mol} = 3.1\times10^{-5}\text{ mol}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Multiplied by molar mass instead of dividing; $n = m/M$, not $m \times M$.

parts.prompt: $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ is highly soluble in water, whereas $\text{NCl}_3$ is nearly insoluble. Explain this observation in terms of the types and relative strengths of the intermolecular forces between each of the solutes and water.
parts.scoring_notes: $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ has N–H bonds and can act as both an H-bond donor (N–H···O in water) and H-bond acceptor (lone pair on N). These strong H-bonds with water are comparable to the H-bonds in liquid water, so $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ dissolves readily ('like dissolves like'). $\text{NCl}_3$ has no N–H bonds (Cl is bonded to N), so it cannot donate H-bonds to water; only weaker dipole-dipole interactions and London dispersion forces exist between $\text{NCl}_3$ and water. These IMFs are weaker than the H-bonds in water that must be broken for dissolution, so $\text{NCl}_3$ is nearly insoluble.

scoring_points.description: For the correct identification of intermolecular forces between each substance and water: 1 point

required_elements: $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ can form hydrogen bonds with water (has N–H bonds as H-bond donors).
$\text{NCl}_3$ cannot form hydrogen bonds with water as an H-bond donor; only weaker dipole-dipole or London dispersion forces.
alternatives.correct_example: $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ has N–H bonds and forms hydrogen bonds with water (N–H···O–H). $\text{NCl}_3$ has no N–H bonds (all bonds are N–Cl); it can only interact with water via weaker dipole-dipole interactions (and London dispersion forces), but cannot donate H-bonds.

wrong_examples: Saying both form equal hydrogen bonds with water — $\text{NCl}_3$ lacks N–H bonds and cannot donate H-bonds.
Saying $\text{NCl}_3$ forms strong H-bonds via the Cl atoms — Cl is not electronegative enough in the context of H-bond donation; N–H, O–H, and F–H are the classic H-bond donors.
common_traps: Hydrogen bonding requires H bonded directly to N, O, or F. $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ has two N–H bonds → H-bond donor. $\text{NCl}_3$ has no N–H bonds → cannot donate H-bonds to water.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct identification of intermolecular forces between each substance and water: 1 point. Accept: both $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ and $\text{NCl}_3$ can participate in hydrogen bonding with water (or both have dipole-dipole attractions to water).

sample_responses.response: $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ has N–H bonds that allow it to form hydrogen bonds with water as an H-bond donor. $\text{NCl}_3$ has no N–H bonds, so it cannot donate H-bonds to water; only dipole-dipole interactions are present.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies different H-bonding capacity; consistent with SG.

sample_responses.response: Both form only London dispersion forces with water.
sample_responses.commentary: Both are polar and can form dipole-dipole interactions with water; $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ also forms H-bonds.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation relating stronger/weaker IMFs with water to higher/lower solubility: 1 point

required_elements: Strong H-bonds between $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ and water facilitate dissolution (energy to break water-water H-bonds is compensated by new $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$-water H-bonds).
Weaker forces between $\text{NCl}_3$ and water cannot compensate for breaking strong water-water H-bonds, so $\text{NCl}_3$ is nearly insoluble.
alternatives.correct_example: For $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$, the strong hydrogen bonds formed between $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ and water are comparable to the H-bonds in liquid water that are broken during dissolution. The energetic cost of disrupting water's H-bond network is offset by the new $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$–water H-bonds, so dissolution is favorable. For $\text{NCl}_3$, the only forces with water are weaker dipole-dipole or dispersion interactions, which cannot compensate for the strong H-bonds in water that must be broken. Dissolution is therefore not energetically favorable, and $\text{NCl}_3$ is nearly insoluble.

wrong_examples: Citing polarity alone without explaining which IMFs form between solute and solvent — must name specific IMF types.
Saying solubility depends on molecular size — molecular size is secondary; the dominant factor here is the type of IMFs with water.
common_traps: Solubility: 'like dissolves like' — polar/H-bonding solutes dissolve in polar/H-bonding solvents (water) because the new solute-solvent interactions compensate for broken solvent-solvent and solute-solute interactions. The key is whether solute-water H-bonds can form.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For a correct explanation: 1 point. The intermolecular forces between $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ molecules and water are stronger than those between $\text{NCl}_3$ molecules and water, leading to the greater solubility of $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ in water.

sample_responses.response: The H-bonds between $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ and water are stronger than the dipole-dipole forces between $\text{NCl}_3$ and water. Stronger solute-water interactions allow $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ to dissolve more readily.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct strength comparison linked to solubility; matches SG.

sample_responses.response: $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ is soluble because it is an ionic compound.
sample_responses.commentary: $\text{NH}_2\text{Cl}$ is a molecular compound, not ionic; solubility is due to its H-bonding ability.

parts.prompt: The value of $\Delta H_{vaporization}$ for $\text{NCl}_3(l)$ is 32.9 kJ/mol. Calculate the amount of energy required to vaporize a 15.0 g sample of $\text{NCl}_3$ (molar mass 120.36 g/mol).

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated energy in kJ: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$n = \dfrac{15.0\text{ g}}{120.36\text{ g/mol}} = 0.1247\text{ mol}$
$q = 0.1247\text{ mol} \times 32.9\text{ kJ/mol} = 4.10\text{ kJ}$

wrong_examples: Multiplying mass by $\Delta H_{vap}$ directly without converting to moles: $15.0 \times 32.9 = 494$ kJ — must convert grams to moles first.
Using molar mass of $\text{NCl}$ (50.46) instead of $\text{NCl}_3$ (120.36) — the compound is $\text{NCl}_3$.
common_traps: $q = n \times \Delta H_{vap}$. Must convert grams to moles using molar mass of $\text{NCl}_3 = 14.01 + 3(35.45) = 120.36$ g/mol.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value: 1 point. $15.0\text{ g} \times (1\text{ mol}/120.36\text{ g}) \times 32.9\text{ kJ/mol} = 4.10\text{ kJ}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Multiplied grams directly by $\Delta H_{vap}$ without converting to moles.
